figures.caption: Figure 4: Ablation on Text-to-SQL (BIRD-147) and Causal Estimation (average MSA). Both tools and knowledge contribute; their combination (Full) achieves the best result. “Pipe.” = Structured Pipeline skill, which underperforms the full adaptive skill.

Figure 2: Task-level overview across all four tasks, averaged over datasets within each task. (a) Performance: the adaptive skill is the best or near-best single-agent method on every task and outperforms Original MAS on CE, CD, and matches on FE. (b) Latency and (c) Cost: single-agent methods are consistently cheaper and faster, with the gap largest on CD (8×) and FE (15×). Per-dataset breakdowns in Appendix F.
Figure 6: Per-datapoint: Metric Freedom F vs. headroom-normalized skill lift (n=13). Error bars show bootstrap σF .
Table 3: Text-to-SQL results on BIRD-147 and Spider-120. EX = Execution Accuracy (%). Best single-agent results in bold. Costs for single-agent methods are cache-adjusted effective costs.

Figure 3: Freedom spectrum. Large filled circles = metric-level aggregates (n=6); small open circles = individual dataset data points (n=13). Dashed lines connect datasets to their metric mean. The negative trend (ρ=−0.62 at the data-point level; ρ=−0.89 at the metric level) confirms that rigid metrics benefit most from skill augmentation. The strongest evidence comes from comparing CEMSA (F≈0, upper-left) and CE-MRE (F≈0.7–0.9, lower-right), computed from the same outputs.
Table 11: Causal Discovery: per-dataset efficiency metrics (averages per dataset). Costs for singleagent methods are cache-adjusted effective costs (USD).
Table 10: Causal Estimation: per-dataset efficiency metrics (averages per query). Costs for singleagent methods are cache-adjusted effective costs (USD).

Table 8: Metric Freedom (F ) and headroom-normalized skill lift across 13 data points, sorted by F . σF = bootstrap standard error; Base = raw-agent score; liftnorm = lift / (1 − base). CE-Synthetic-MSA is excluded: with 97.8% raw accuracy, no mixed questions remain after filtering.
Table 9: Causal Discovery: complete metrics for all methods and datasets (mean ± std over 3 runs).

Figure 5: Skill iterator trajectories across all four tasks. Val score (solid) and Train score (dashed) per iteration. Green dashed line marks the selected best version. (a) CE-MSA (F≈0): rapid gain to 100% at v2, then oscillation. (b) CD (F=0.24–0.77): v2 achieves best val F1 (90.9%); v1 timed out. (c) T2SQL (F=0.50): steady improvement, val EX plateaus at 86.7% from v3. (d) FE (F≈0.59–0.97): marginal gains, val AUC plateaus near 65.6% after v2, consistent with high freedom.
Table 4: Causal Estimation results. MSA (%) higher is better; MRE (%) lower is better. Cost = avg cost per query (cache-adjusted for single-agent methods). Best single-agent results in bold.

Figure 7: Full per-dataset breakdown. Row 1: performance (EX% / MSA% / F1×100 / AUC×100). Row 2: cache-hit cost (linear scale; T2SQL/CE per-query, CD/FE per-run). Row 3: latency (log scale). Single-agent methods benefit from prompt caching (≈30–40% of uncached cost); Original MAS baselines do not. †FE Original MAS cost estimated: Taobao $7.5–$15, Dia $10–$22 per run.

Figure 1: System overview. Our two-stage framework converts multi-agent systems into efficient single-agent skills guided by Metric Freedom F = 1− ρ(Dout, Dscore). Stage 1 selectively extracts tools and knowledge; task-decomposition structure is retained or discarded based on F . Stage 2 optionally iterates the skill via a four-agent architecture (Explore, Main, Analyzer, Runner), recommended when F ≲ 0.6.
